title: Collaborative Git & GitHub
content:
## 🤝 Collaborative Git: Working in Teams Without Chaos

Real-world software is built in teams. Git's branching and collaboration model is what makes this possible without developers overwriting each other's work. This lesson covers everything you need to collaborate professionally on GitHub.

---

## 1️⃣ Branches: Parallel Timelines

A **branch** is an independent copy of the codebase where you can work without affecting the main codebase. Think of it as a parallel timeline.

```bash
git branch                    # List all local branches
git branch feature-login      # Create a new branch
git checkout feature-login    # Switch to it
git checkout -b feature-login # Create AND switch (shortcut)
git switch -c feature-login   # Modern alternative
```

The main branch is typically called `main` (or historically `master`).

---

## 2️⃣ Merging: Combining Timelines

When a feature is complete, you merge it back into `main`:

```bash
git checkout main
git merge feature-login
```

**Merge Conflict**: If two people edited the same line of the same file, Git can't decide which to keep. You must manually resolve the conflict, then commit.

Conflict markers in the file:
```
<<<<<<< HEAD (your changes)
const title = "Hello"
=======
const title = "Hi"
>>>>>>> feature-login (incoming changes)
```

---

## 3️⃣ Forking

**Forking** creates a personal copy of someone else's repository under YOUR GitHub account. You use it to propose changes to open-source projects:

1. Fork the repo on GitHub
2. Clone YOUR fork locally
3. Make changes on a branch
4. Push to your fork
5. Create a Pull Request to the original repo

---

## 4️⃣ Cloning & Remotes

```bash
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git  # Download a repo
git remote -v                               # View remotes (fetch/push URLs)
git remote add upstream https://...         # Add original repo as 'upstream'
```

- **`origin`**: The default remote — usually your fork on GitHub
- **`upstream`**: Convention for the original repo you forked from

---

## 5️⃣ Push & Pull

```bash
git push origin feature-login   # Upload your branch to GitHub
git pull origin main             # Download + merge remote changes
git fetch origin                 # Download changes WITHOUT merging
```

**`git pull` = `git fetch` + `git merge`**

---

## 6️⃣ Pull Requests (PRs)

A **Pull Request** is a proposal to merge your branch into another branch (usually `main`). It triggers:
- Code review by teammates
- Automated tests (CI/CD)
- Discussion and feedback

On GitHub: New PR → select base branch (main) → select compare branch (your feature) → describe changes → Submit.

---

## 7️⃣ Rebase vs Merge

| | Merge | Rebase |
|---|---|---|
| **History** | Keeps all history, adds "merge commit" | Creates linear history, re-applies your commits |
| **Use case** | Preserving full history | Keeping a clean, linear history |
| **Danger** | Less risky | ⚠️ Never rebase public branches |

```bash
git rebase main    # Move your commits on top of main's latest commit
```

---

## 8️⃣ Git Stash

When you need to switch branches but have unfinished work:

```bash
git stash           # Save current uncommitted changes temporarily
git stash pop       # Restore the saved changes
git stash list      # View all stashes
```

---

## 9️⃣ Deleting Branches

```bash
git branch -d feature-done    # Delete local branch (safe — only if merged)
git branch -D feature-done    # Force delete (even if not merged)
git push origin --delete feature-done  # Delete remote branch on GitHub
```

---

## 🔟 GitHub Actions (CI/CD)

**GitHub Actions** automates tasks when you push code:
- Run tests automatically on every PR
- Build and deploy your app to production
- Send notifications or create releases

```yaml
# .github/workflows/test.yml
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - run: npm install && npm test
```

---

## 💡 Professional Workflow (Trunk-Based Development)

1. Always branch off `main`
2. Work in short-lived feature branches (< 1-2 days ideal)
3. Open a PR when done
4. Get code reviewed
5. Merge after approval
6. Delete the branch

questions.question_text: Which command creates a new branch and switches to it?
questions.explanation: Both 'git checkout -b' (classic) and 'git switch -c' (modern) are used specifically for this purpose.

questions.question_text: What command is used to download an existing repository from GitHub to your local machine?
questions.explanation: git clone creates a full copy of the remote repository and its history.

options: A URL pointing to a version of your project on a server (like GitHub)
A branch that has been deleted
questions.explanation: Remotes allow you to sync your local work with a shared repository.

questions.question_text: Which command sends your local commits to GitHub?
questions.explanation: Pushing 'updates' the remote branch with your local changes.

questions.question_text: Which command fetches changes from GitHub AND merges them into your local branch?
questions.explanation: git pull is essentially 'git fetch' followed by 'git merge'.

options: A proposal to merge changes from one branch into another in a collaborative setting
A way to delete a branch
questions.explanation: PRs are the heart of collaboration on platforms like GitHub.

questions.question_text: How do you create a new branch named 'feature-login'?
questions.explanation: 'git branch' creates it. 'git checkout -b' creates it AND switches to it.

options: Git deletes both versions of the file
Git cannot automatically decide which changes to keep and needs a human to fix it
questions.explanation: Conflicts happen when two people change the same line of the same file.

questions.question_text: Which command switches you from the 'main' branch to a 'dev' branch?
questions.explanation: Checkout (or 'git switch') updates your files to match the target branch.

options: Creating a personal copy of someone else's repo on your GitHub account
Running two versions of Git at once
questions.explanation: Forking lets you experiment on a project without affecting the original.

questions.question_text: In a 'Rebase', what happens to your commits?
options: They are moved to the end of the target branch's history
They are sent to the trash
questions.explanation: Rebase creates a cleaner, linear history by moving your base commit.

questions.question_text: What is the standard name of the main remote on most repositories?
questions.explanation: Origin is the default alias for the URL you cloned from.

questions.question_text: How do you delete a local branch named 'old-feature'?
questions.explanation: The '-d' flag safely deletes a branch that has been merged.

questions.question_text: Which GitHub feature allows you to automate tasks like testing or deployment?
questions.explanation: Actions allow you to run 'workflows' on your code automatically.

options: Temporarily hiding uncommitted changes so you can switch branches
questions.explanation: Stashing is like a 'pause button' for your current work.

questions.question_text: Which command is used to see which remotes are connected to your local repo?
questions.explanation: The '-v' (verbose) flag shows the fetch and push URLs.
